subject: ap-precalculus
title: Exponential Decay Modeling: Radioactive Isotope & Semi-Log Analysis
stimulus:
A laboratory sample of a radioactive isotope initially contains $500$ milligrams. Scientists measure the remaining amount at various times (in hours) and record the following data:

| Time $t$ (hours) | Amount $A$ (mg) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 500 |
| 10 | 341 |
| 25 | 191 |
| 40 | 107 |
| 60 | 43 |

A scientist proposes the model $A(t) = a \cdot b^t$ to represent the amount remaining, in milligrams, at time $t$ hours.

parts.prompt:
(i) Use the data at $t = 0$ and $t = 60$ to write two equations that can be used to find the values for constants $a$ and $b$ in the expression for $A(t)$.

(ii) Find the values for $a$ and $b$ as decimal approximations. Show the computations that lead to your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: The first equation immediately gives $a = 500$ since $b^0 = 1$. For $b$: $500 \cdot b^{60} = 43 \Rightarrow b = (43/500)^{1/60} \approx 0.963$. Decimal accuracy: 3 places. Accept $b$ in $[0.962, 0.964]$. Full credit for $A(t) = 500(0.963)^t$ as a complete response for both points. Partial credit (1 for A1, 0 for A2) if one correct equation and one correct value are given.

scoring_points.description: Two correct equations using the given data: $a \cdot b^0 = 500$ (giving $a = 500$) and $500 \cdot b^{60} = 43$
wrong_examples: Using incorrect data points such as $A(10)$ instead of $A(60)$
common_traps: The problem specifies using $t = 0$ and $t = 60$ — using other data points may yield different $b$ values and will not earn the equations point
scoring_points.official_rubric: The point is earned for writing two correct equations using the specified data points $t = 0$ and $t = 60$: $a \cdot b^0 = 500$ (immediately giving $a = 500$) and $500 \cdot b^{60} = 43$. Using data points other than $t = 0$ and $t = 60$ does not earn the point.

sample_responses.commentary: Correctly applies the model at the two specified data points, deduces $a = 500$ from the zero-exponent term, and writes the correct second equation using $t = 60$.
Uses valid data but the problem specifies using $t = 0$ and $t = 60$. Equations from other data pairs do not earn the equations point.
Alternative acceptable form — states $a = 500$ directly (implied by $b^0 = 1$) and writes the correct second equation. Both required equations are present.

scoring_points.description: Correct values $a = 500$ and $b \approx 0.963$ from $b = (43/500)^{1/60}$; supporting work must show the root-taking or logarithm step
wrong_examples: $b = 0.96$ (fewer than 3 decimal places — decimal presentation error)
common_traps: Decimal presentation error: must give at least three decimal places
Forgetting to take the $n$th root — $b^{60} = 0.086$ must be solved for $b$, not left as $b = 0.086$
scoring_points.official_rubric: The point is earned for $a = 500$ and $b \approx 0.963$ (accept $[0.962, 0.964]$) with supporting work showing the root-taking or logarithm step. Decimal approximations must be correct to three places after the decimal point. The first decimal presentation error per question does not earn the point.

sample_responses.commentary: Correctly isolates $b^{60}$, takes the $60$th root, and reports $b$ to three decimal places alongside $a = 500$.
Correctly divides to isolate $b^{60}$ but confuses this ratio with $b$ itself — the $60$th root must be taken to solve for $b \approx 0.963$.
Sets up the root correctly but reports the answer to only two decimal places; this is a decimal presentation error and does not earn the point on its first occurrence.

parts.prompt:
(i) Find the average rate of change of $A$, in milligrams per hour, from $t = 10$ to $t = 40$. Express your answer as a decimal approximation. Show the computations that lead to your answer.

(ii) Explain what the average rate of change found in part (i) means in the context of this problem.
parts.scoring_notes: For part (i), $\frac{107 - 341}{40 - 10} = \frac{-234}{30} = -7.8$ mg/hr (exact). Supporting work must show the quotient. For part (ii), all three components must be present: (a) the amount is decreasing, (b) at a rate of approximately $7.8$ mg/hr, and (c) over the interval from $t = 10$ to $t = 40$ hours. Omitting the time interval or the decreasing direction does not earn the point.

scoring_points.description: Correct average rate of change $\frac{107 - 341}{40 - 10} = -7.8$ milligrams per hour, in presence of a quotient using the data values
wrong_examples: $\frac{341 - 107}{30} = 7.8$ (wrong sign — isotope is decaying, rate must be negative)
common_traps: Supporting work showing the difference quotient is required
Units not needed but rate must be negative to reflect decay
scoring_points.official_rubric: The point is earned for the correct average rate of change of $-7.8$ milligrams per hour with supporting work showing the difference quotient $\frac{107 - 341}{40 - 10}$. The negative sign is required to reflect that the quantity is decreasing.

sample_responses.commentary: Correctly sets up the difference quotient with the right data values, subtracts in the correct order, and arrives at $-7.8$ milligrams per hour.
Subtracts in the wrong order, producing a positive rate. For a decaying quantity the average rate of change must be negative; a positive value does not earn the point.
Computes the change in $A$ but does not divide by the change in $t$ ($30$ hours). The average rate of change requires a division by the interval length.

scoring_points.description: Contextual interpretation with all three components: direction (decreasing), rate ($7.8$ mg/hr), and time interval ($t = 10$ to $t = 40$ hours)

alternatives.correct_example: On average, the radioactive sample lost $7.8$ milligrams per hour between $10$ and $40$ hours after the start of the experiment.

wrong_examples: 'The isotope decreases' without referencing the rate or time interval
Stating the rate without mentioning the direction or context
common_traps: All three components — direction, rate, and time interval — must be present to earn the point
scoring_points.official_rubric: The point is earned for a contextual interpretation that includes all three required components: (1) the amount is decreasing, (2) at a rate of approximately $7.8$ mg/hr, and (3) over the interval from $t = 10$ to $t = 40$ hours. Omitting any one component does not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: Between $10$ and $40$ hours after the start of the experiment, the radioactive sample lost an average of $7.8$ milligrams per hour.
sample_responses.commentary: Includes all three required components: the decreasing direction (lost), the rate ($7.8$ mg/hr), and the time interval ($t = 10$ to $t = 40$ hours).

sample_responses.response: The isotope is decreasing at $7.8$ mg/hr.
sample_responses.commentary: Includes direction and rate but omits the specific time interval $t = 10$ to $t = 40$ hours. All three components are required.

sample_responses.response: Between $t = 10$ and $t = 40$ hours, the sample is losing mass.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies direction and time interval but omits the rate ($7.8$ mg/hr). All three components must be present.

parts.prompt:
(i) Use the data at $t = 0$ and $t = 60$ to find the slope of the line on a semi-log plot of $\ln(A)$ versus $t$. Show the computations that lead to your answer.

(ii) A different isotope is plotted on a similar semi-log plot of $\ln(A)$ versus $t$, and the data appear approximately linear with a steeper negative slope. What does a steeper negative slope indicate about that isotope compared to the original?
parts.scoring_notes: For part (i), slope $= \frac{\ln(43) - \ln(500)}{60 - 0} = \frac{3.761 - 6.215}{60} \approx -0.041$ (accept $[-0.042, -0.040]$). Note this equals $\ln(b)$ from part (a), consistent. For part (ii), a steeper negative slope means $\ln(b)$ is more negative, so $b$ is smaller, meaning a faster rate of decay and shorter half-life. Award the point for connecting steeper slope to faster decay or shorter half-life.

scoring_points.description: Correct slope $\frac{\ln(43) - \ln(500)}{60 - 0} \approx -0.041$; supporting work must show the quotient using $\ln$ of the data values
common_traps: The semi-log plot uses $\ln(A)$ on the vertical axis and $t$ on the horizontal — slope is the change in $\ln(A)$ over the change in $t$, not the raw change in $A$
scoring_points.official_rubric: The point is earned for a correct slope $\approx -0.041$ (accept $[-0.042, -0.040]$) with supporting work showing the quotient $\frac{\ln(43) - \ln(500)}{60 - 0}$. Using raw $A$ values instead of their natural logarithms does not earn the point.

sample_responses.commentary: Correctly computes the natural logarithms of the $A$ values, forms the difference quotient with $\Delta t = 60$, and arrives at $-0.041$.
Uses the raw $A$ values rather than their natural logarithms. On a semi-log plot the vertical axis is $\ln(A)$, so the slope must be computed with logarithms.
Correctly computes the numerator ($\ln(43) - \ln(500) = \ln(43/500)$) but forgets to divide by $\Delta t = 60$; the slope must be the ratio of vertical change to horizontal change.

scoring_points.description: Correct interpretation: a steeper negative slope on the semi-log plot indicates a faster rate of decay and shorter half-life for the second isotope

required_elements: faster rate of decay OR shorter half-life
alternatives.correct_example: A steeper negative slope indicates the second isotope decays faster — it loses a larger fraction of its mass per hour, giving it a shorter half-life than the original isotope.

wrong_examples: Stating 'slower decay' — incorrect, steeper (more negative) slope means faster decay
Describing slope in terms of the graph appearance without connecting to decay rate
common_traps: On a semi-log plot, the slope equals $\ln(b)$; a more negative slope means a smaller base $b$, which means faster exponential decay
scoring_points.official_rubric: The point is earned for an interpretation that connects a steeper negative slope on the semi-log plot to a faster rate of decay or shorter half-life for the second isotope. Describing only the graph's appearance without connecting to the decay context does not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: A steeper negative slope means $\ln(b)$ is more negative, so the base $b$ is smaller. A smaller $b$ means the isotope loses a larger fraction of its mass per hour, so it decays faster and has a shorter half-life.
sample_responses.commentary: Connects the steeper slope explicitly to the decay rate constant ($\ln b$), correctly infers a smaller $b$, and links that to faster decay and shorter half-life.

sample_responses.response: A steeper negative slope means the line falls more quickly on the graph.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes only the graphical appearance without connecting the slope to the decay rate or half-life of the isotope. A contextual interpretation about the decay rate is required.

sample_responses.response: A steeper negative slope means the second isotope decays more slowly because the line drops less time.
sample_responses.commentary: Incorrect direction — a steeper (more negative) slope corresponds to faster, not slower, decay. The slope equals $\ln(b)$; a more negative value means a smaller $b$ and a shorter half-life.
